<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected

<insights>

- High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected
- Strong cohesion (0.87) with low coupling (0.13) indicates a well-bounded subsystem — usage tracking is correctly isolated from routing and viewer concerns
- The scheduler pattern (register-scheduler, usage-cleanup-scheduler) cleanly separates registration from execution logic, making it easy to modify scheduling behavior independently
- shared-types.ts and task-usage.ts as dual entry points provide a clean internal layering: types are defined separately from the usage gateway that consumers depend on
- High cohesion (0.87) with low coupling (0.13) is a strong architectural signal — this subsystem is well encapsulated and minimizes blast radius for changes to usage tracking logic.
- 3 imports flow from web-application-core into this zone alongside 37 flowing the other direction — the 3 reverse imports from a higher-level zone into this subsystem may indicate a layering inversion and should be reviewed to confirm directionality is intentional.
- Co-locating production source files and unit tests within the same cohesive zone (rather than a separate test-only zone) is appropriate here given the tight functional grouping and makes coverage gaps immediately visible.
- The 37 inbound cross-zone calls from web-application-core and 9 outbound calls from this zone back into web-application-core represent the only near-symmetric call exchange in the non-messaging parts of the web package — all other zones exchange calls asymmetrically
- The 3 reverse imports from web-application-core into task-usage-scheduler have not been confirmed as layering inversions — they may be type-only imports from shared-types.ts which is the zone's intentional public interface
- The 3 reverse imports flowing from web-application-core into task-usage-scheduler likely target shared-types.ts, which is the zone's intentional public type surface. If confirmed as type-only imports, this is correct layering — the composition root reads types from a subsystem without importing implementation. Verify with a targeted grep before treating as a layering inversion.
- With 84 inbound call-graph calls from web-application-core and 9 outbound, the task-usage-scheduler is primarily a consumed subsystem with minimal back-referencing — a healthy asymmetric producer/consumer pattern that aligns with its role as a bounded usage-tracking subsystem.
- task-usage.ts is classified with [gateway] archetype but is located inside a subsystem zone rather than at the package boundary. All other gateways in the codebase (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts, external.ts, api.ts) live at zone edges. If task-usage.ts imports from the rex package directly, it creates a second ungoverned rex import path outside src/server/rex-gateway.ts, making the rex import surface no longer fully auditable from a single file.
- The zone's test-to-production file ratio (8 test files to 7 production files, ratio 1.14:1) is the highest in the web package — this subsystem is the most thoroughly unit-tested server zone and represents the target test density that higher-risk zones like web-viewer should aspire to.
- task-usage.ts carries a [gateway] archetype label inside a subsystem zone rather than at the canonical package boundary. If it imports rex symbols directly rather than through src/server/rex-gateway.ts, it violates the one-gateway-per-source-package rule and creates a second ungoverned cross-package rex import path that bypasses the auditable gateway surface.
- Zone name 'task-usage-scheduler' elevates the scheduling mechanism to primary status over the domain concern (usage tracking). In all other multi-word zone names in this codebase, the domain noun leads (rex-fix-command, viewer-messaging-pipeline, web-data-routes, web-landing-page). Prioritizing the mechanism makes the zone harder to locate when reasoning about 'where does task usage data live' — a contributor searching for usage tracking logic will scan for 'task-usage' and find the zone, but one searching for 'scheduler' will find it too, creating ambiguous ownership of the scheduler mechanism vs. the usage domain.
- task-usage-scheduler co-locates 8 unit test files with 7 production source files (1.14:1 ratio) while all other server-side production zones (routes, gateways, services) place their unit tests in a separate zone (web-unit tests/ directory). This zone is the sole exception to the server test placement convention — an implicit split that means the web-unit zone does not provide a complete picture of server-side unit test coverage.
- Rename the zone from 'task-usage-scheduler' to 'task-usage' or 'task-usage-tracking' to reflect the domain boundary (usage data management) rather than the implementation mechanism (scheduling). The scheduling approach is an implementation detail that can change without changing the zone's domain identity — naming after the mechanism instead of the domain reduces discoverability and conflates what with how.
- This zone co-locates unit tests with production source while all other server-side zones place unit tests in a separate zone (web-unit). Document which convention is intentional. If co-location is preferred (the test-to-source ratio here is the highest in the web package), migrate other server unit tests to co-locate rather than aggregating in web-unit — a mixed convention makes server-side test coverage reporting inconsistent across zones.
- [call graph] 219 internal calls, 9 outgoing, 88 incoming (cohesion: 0.96, coupling: 0.04)

</insights>
